narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to predict the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 (slightly above the average of 3.21), LSAT score of 33.50 (below the typical 36.70), Year 1 school decile of 5 (close to the average 5.71), Year 3 decile of 4 (below the average 5.53), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 49.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, family income level, and LSAT score, each pulling the prediction in key directions. Most critically, your Year 3 decile of 4—the ranking of your grades within your school that year—stood out as the top driver, strongly pushing the model toward predicting bar failure because it signals a dip in performance compared to peers. Your middle family income bracket was the next biggest influence, also nudging the prediction toward failure, as the model associates this level with slightly higher risk profiles in the dataset. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 33.50 had the strongest counterbalancing effect, pushing toward a pass prediction despite being below average, showing it helped mitigate some concerns. Overall, while your undergraduate GPA and full-time status were favorable or typical, they couldn't outweigh these top concerns, placing your profile among those with moderate-to-higher failure risk relative to admitted students who typically show stronger late-stage grades and higher test scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with solid early academics—many in similar demographics succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic performance through additional coursework or explanations for the Year 3 dip, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that weigh your full story. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors or prepare a stronger personal statement highlighting resilience, as reapplying with these enhancements could shift your predicted odds significantly. Keep pushing; your above-average undergrad record shows you have the foundation to turn this around.
